dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for cardiac arrest at 1234 Maple Street.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what's going on here?
She's not breathing and has no pulse. The firefighters are doing compressions right now.
AVPU is unresponsive. Eyes closed, no response to any stimulus.
Eyes remain closed and unresponsive to gentle and painful stimuli.
No verbal response to questions or shouting the patient's name.
No motor movement with noxious stimulus applied to both sides of the body.
Airway is clear with no signs of obstruction based on visual inspection.
Spontaneous breathing is absent. No chest rise or fall observed.
Carotid pulse is absent. Radial is not palpable. Skin is warm and moist.
Blood pressure is unobtainable with manual assessment. Estimated around 0/0.
Respiratory rate is not measurable. Patient is apneic at the moment.
Pulse oximetry shows 0%. No signal detected.
Glucose level is 212 mg/dL as tested from a finger stick.
Monitor shows PEA - Pulseless Electrical Activity is present.
Continuing manual chest compressions per standing protocols.
We're using a BVM with 15 liters of oxygen for ventilations.
Inserting a nasopharyngeal airway into the left nare now.
NPA inserted with no resistance observed in the nasal canal.
Intraosseous access is placed at the left greater tubercle.
Administering first dose of epinephrine 1:10,000 at 1 mg.
The family says she's been tired and lightheaded for about a week.
She has diabetes, hypertension, hypothyroidism, and mental health issues.
Her medications include Invega, Synthroid, metformin, and Glypizide.
No pain response given the GCS of 3. No verbal or physical cues evident.
Activating cardiac arrest protocol 2-7 with full ALS intercept.
Rate is maintained at 100 to 120 compressions per minute.
Second epinephrine dose administered through our intraosseous access.
Patient prepared for oral intubation. King tube is being used.
Oral intubation successful with a size 4 King LT SD placed.
50 mEq of sodium bicarbonate given IO after ensuring tube placement.
First IO line is accidentally dislodged once we moved her from the floor.
A second intraosseous line placed at the left tibial tuberosity as needed.
Third epinephrine dose delivered after resecure IO in new location.
Baseline rhythm still PEA with no improvement in EKG or perfusion signs.
Fourth epinephrine dose delivered. Airway and compressions continue strong.
Secretions in airway - suction initiated through OG tube to improve ET compliance.
Arranging to transport to VCU Medical Center for advanced cardiac care.
Patient is secured with 3 seat belts. All straps are tight and should not loosen.
VCU is being updated on our incoming cardiac arrest with ongoing PEA.
Confirming all secure status. Ready for immediate transport to hospital.
